title: Quantum Compression for Distributed Entanglement
authors: Jan Østergaard, Shashi Raj Pandey, Christophe Biscio, Torben Bach Pedersen, Petar Popovski
abstract: We study compression strategies for multipartite entanglement distribution under uncertainty in the partitioning of the quantum state. When the partition is not known at the time of state preparation, we show that a joint design of the resource state and a family of compression schemes can increase the entanglement across partitions under a fixed transmission budget. We formulate this as a source coding problem and derive non-asymptotic upper and lower bounds on the achievable average entanglement subject to an average coding rate. We furthermore design an efficient method for jointly optimizing states and lossless compression maps by exploiting the inherent symmetry of weighted Dicke states. In the bipartite case, we propose practical constructions that closely approach the derived upper bound, and more generally we provide practical constructions for multipartite settings.
summary: The paper presents a method to jointly optimize quantum states and compression schemes to maximize entanglement distribution in networks with uncertain topologies. It provides mathematical bounds on entanglement achievable under a fixed transmission budget and demonstrates that compression can be beneficial even in the presence of noisy hardware.
main_problem: Optimizing the distribution of multipartite entanglement in quantum networks when the partitioning of qubits among parties is unknown at the time of state preparation.
main_result: The authors derive non-asymptotic upper and lower bounds on achievable average entanglement and identify a crossover point where compression benefits outweigh the noise introduced by compression circuits.
method: A joint optimization framework using quantum source coding theory, symmetry-based optimization of weighted Dicke states, and local lossless isometric compression maps.
model_or_system: A system of n qubits distributed among m parties, where the assignment of qubits to parties is random. The states are restricted to the permutation-invariant (symmetric) subspace, such as Dicke and Comb states.
key_observables: Average entanglement (Geometric Multipartite Entanglement and von Neumann entropy), average coding rate, and Logarithmic Negativity under noise.
important_parameters: Average coding rate (transmission budget), number of qubits (n), number of parties (m), and channel depolarizing probability (p).
main_assumptions: The compression is lossless for states in the symmetric subspace, and the assignment of qubits to parties is independent of the prepared state.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the compression of subsystems to reduce communication costs; Figure 2 and 3 show entanglement-rate curves for bipartite and multipartite cases; Figure 4 compares uncompressed, ideal, and noisy compression performance under channel noise.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of entanglement distribution under uncertainty, formulates it as a quantum source coding problem, derives analytical bounds for bipartite and multipartite settings, proposes an efficient optimization method using Dicke states, and evaluates performance under circuit and channel noise.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum network design and open quantum systems, as it addresses the practical trade-off between reducing channel noise exposure via compression and the error introduction from noisy quantum gates.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. An n = 7 qubit symmetric state |ϕ⟩is prepared. Three randomly chosen qubits are selected for Alice and the remaining four for Bob.
Fig. 2. Entanglement-rate curves for m = 2 and n = 2, . . . , 15. The upper and lower bounds are given by Theorems 1 and 2, respectively.
Fig. 3. Entanglement-rate curves for m = 3 and n = 3, . . . , 15. The upper and lower bounds are given by Theorems 3 and 4, respectively.
